data.title: VSS: Why I Keeep Buying International Small Caps
data.description:
Hunting for alpha? Have you taken a look at international small caps? The Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US Small-Cap Index Fund ETF Shares (NYSEARCA:VSS) is up 14 of the past 16 weeks. An incredible rally, VSS is +34% from its April low, outperforming both the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and the Vanguard FTSE Developed Markets ETF (VEA). The foreign small-cap product has also topped the Vanguard Emerging Markets ETF (VWO) by more than four percentage points.

I have a sizable position in VSS. It’s a long-term holding for me, but I have never covered it on Seeking Alpha. I have a buy rating—not because I own it, but given its strong momentum and attractive valuation. Let’s get to it.

VSS Beating Other Broad-Market Index ETFs Last 6 Months

According to the issuer, VSS provides a convenient way to get broad exposure across developed and emerging non-U.S. small-cap equity markets around the world. It aims to track the performance of the FTSE Global Small Cap ex US Index.

VSS is a medium-sized ETF with $11.4 billion in assets under management as of July 24, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is very low at just eight basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 2.8%. I hold the fund in both taxable and tax-advantaged accounts. Though the yield is high, investors can capture the Foreign Tax Credit by holding it in a regular brokerage account, given the high yield.

Share-price momentum is just about the best it has ever been, at an A- ETF Grade from Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. VSS also scores well regarding risk, thanks to its diversified portfolio and somewhat tame historical standard deviation trends. Finally, liquidity is generally healthy, although I often wait until the final hour of trading each day when I purchase the fund, since the bid/ask spread tends to be quite tight then.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the 3-star, Gold-rated ETF by Morningstar is a rare international fund with virtually no large-cap exposure. Moreover, there a nice mix of small- and mid-cap stocks, per Morningstar’s classification. So, it’s not as speculative as you might think. Finally, value, blend, and growth are all well represented in the allocation.

From a valuation perspective, VSS trades at a very compelling 13.2x price-to-earnings ratio. Considering its high 16.6% long-term EPS growth rate, it’s hard not to think the fund will deliver competitive returns in the next decade.

VSS: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As for the sector exposure, there’s also a nice mix of positions. Industrials is the largest weight, while Information Technology is 12% of the ETF. Financials is not as big a stake as you might imagine, which helps to offset bank-heavy ex-US products.

Communication Services is a relative underweight to the S&P 500, as is Consumer Staples. Real Estate, which has dramatically underperformed in the past five years, is an overweight, along with Materials.

VSS: Holdings & Dividend Information

By now, you might think a “Strong Buy” is in the cards, but seasonal headwinds temper my near-term expectations. Over the past 10 years, the August through October stretch has been bearish. So, after a tremendous rally in the past three-plus months, a consolidation would certainly make sense.

VSS: Bearish Short-Run Seasonality

The Technical Take

With a low valuation, high EPS growth, YTD alpha, and bearish calendar trends, VSS’s technical chart is strong. Notice in the graph below that shares are within earshot of the 2021 all-time high. Of course, on a total return basis, VSS is already in uncharted territory. I also like that the RSI momentum oscillator has confirmed the price advance, while the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also take a look at the volume by price profile on the left side of the graph—there’s not much overhead supply of shares to prevent the bulls here. I see support down at the previous breakout level of $126-$127, while resistance is near $142.

VSS: All-Time High In Sight, $142 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on VSS. It’s about 12% of my overall portfolio (my entire net worth), and after a long period of underperformance to the US stock market, I expect strong returns over the decade ahead. Hot 2025 momentum is a great start.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - U.S. Data Dump, Earnings Season And Trade Deals
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in review: Trade Deals Materialize

The August 1 tariff deadline approaches, and with it we have had a few trade deal announcements that came out this week. Market sentiment seemed to get a boost, with gold in particular feeling the heat of a stronger US dollar.

Market participants remain at least partially on the edge of their seats, as we have not seen any details of agreements as yet. This led to early signs of cracks in potential trade deals with the US announcing a Japan trade deal that included significant investments in the US.

However, Japanese officials and US officials seem to have differing views of the deal, with Japanese officials stating that the US will secure only 90 percent of profits from joint investments with Japan if it takes on a proportional amount of risk and financing. This seems to suggest that cracks may be present in the two allies’ interpretation of their hastily agreed trade deal.

Japanese officials further stressed there was no written agreement with Washington and no legally binding one would be drawn up after Trump administration officials claimed Tokyo would back investments in the US from which American taxpayers would reap nine-tenths of the profits.

Wall Street and the dollar strengthened on Friday as investors prepared for the upcoming week. All three major indexes were slightly up in early trading and set to end the week with gains.

The week also saw global investors snap up a net $8.71 billion worth of equity funds during the week, reversing a $4.4 billion net withdrawal in the prior week, data from LSEG Lipper showed.

With just a week left before Trump's trade deadline, the US and its partners are rushing to finalize deals. European negotiators are optimistic after the trade agreement with Japan earlier this week.

The dollar strengthened but is still set for its biggest monthly drop as investors focus on upcoming trade talks and central bank meetings. The dollar index rose 0.28% to 97.72, while the euro fell 0.2% to $1.173. Against the yen, the dollar gained 0.4%, reaching 147.57.

In cryptocurrencies, bitcoin dropped 3.08% to $115,133.22, and Ethereum fell 2.63% to $3,641.43.

Oil prices dipped as investors considered global demand and a possible supply increase from Venezuela. US crude fell 0.56% to $65.63 per barrel, and Brent dropped 0.39% to $68.91.

Gold prices also declined as the stronger dollar and optimism over US-EU trade talks reduced demand for the safe-haven metal. Spot gold fell 0.93% to $3,336.52 an ounce.

Earnings Season

Over a third of S&P 500 companies have reported earnings, with 80% beating expectations, according to LSEG data. Analysts now predict second quarter earnings will grow 7.7% compared to the 5.8% estimate from July 1.

Next week, four big tech companies Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), and Microsoft (MSFT) will release their earnings. Investors will closely watch their updates to see if spending on AI is delivering results and if trade tariffs are still affecting their future plans.

The Week Ahead: US Very Much in Focus, Fed Decision, Trade Deals and Earnings

The week ahead has several important data releases lined up. The US and UK will release inflation data with key GDP data from China and manufacturing data from Japan.

Asia Pacific Markets - US/China Trade Talks

The key focus this week is the US-China trade talks in Sweden. A 90-day tariff ceasefire, which started in May, is set to end on August 12. Markets are watching closely to see if the ceasefire will be extended or if there will be changes to current tariffs. An agreement is expected, but uncertainty remains despite President Trump's claims that a framework is in place.

On the data front, China's official July PMI (out Thursday) is expected to stay in contraction at 49.6. The S&P PMI (focused on private and export-driven firms) will follow on Friday. Over the weekend, June industrial profits data will be released. After a sharp drop in May, markets are eager to see if profits recover due to eased trade tensions or if the decline continues.

The Bank of Japan (BoJ) is not expected to make any changes at its meeting on July 30-31. However, markets will pay attention to the BoJ's updated economic outlook. The recent US-Japan trade deal has reduced uncertainty, which may ease pressure on the BoJ.

If inflation forecasts are raised, it could give clues about future interest rates. On the downside, weak industrial production data for June may hurt growth, but this could be balanced by a rebound in retail sales.

Economic Data from Europe, UK and the US

The US will be a key focus next week thanks to a data dump and, of course, trade deal announcements.

On Wednesday, we will get 2nd quarter GDP data, which I expect to grow 3.3% (above the 2.5% forecast), driven by strong trade and investment. However, consumer spending, a key growth driver, has slowed since late 2024 due to tariff concerns and economic uncertainty.

This will be followed by the Fed rate decision later in the day. We obviously have the ongoing attacks at Fed Chair Jerome Powell by US President Trump and his administration.

However, this is unlikely to sway the Fed at this stage, as they are likely to adopt a wait and see approach. The economy is slowing but stable. The Fed is unlikely to cut rates now, though I could see a 50 bp cut in December if inflation eases. The US data week will end with a focus on Jobs data and PCE.

The Fed's preferred inflation measure, the core PCE deflator, is expected to rise 0.3% in June, slightly higher than CPI's 0.2%. NFP data on Friday is expected at 100-120k, with unemployment ticking up to 4.2%.

As Europe heads into summer, key eurozone data is due. GDP is expected to slow after a strong 1Q boosted by U.S. trade activity. April saw drops in production and exports, though May had a rebound, especially in pharmaceuticals. Overall, U.S. developments likely hurt eurozone GDP, and weak service sector performance may add to the slowdown.

On Friday we will get Euro area inflation data. ECB President Lagarde has highlighted stable inflation and steady growth as positives. July's inflation data is expected to stay calm, but the focus will be on the U.S.-EU trade relationship as the August 1 deadline nears.

Chart of the Week - Gold (XAU/USD)

This week's chart of the week is gold (XAU/USD). Gold has shrugged off its early week gains and dropped over $100 from the weekly highs around the $3440/oz mark.

Looking at the chart below, we have the triangle pattern, which appeared to experience an upside breakout earlier in the week before reversing now to test the lower band of the triangle pattern.

Gold has been mixed since making fresh all-time highs in April of $3500/oz with higher highs followed by lower lows. However, the failure this week to take out the most recent swing high at $3451/oz on June 16 may warrant caution for bulls.

A break below the lower end of the triangle pattern would usually be a sign of further downside; however, following the false breakout this week, market participants may rightly be slightly confused.

If the trendline holds, bulls may return; however, if it does give way, then the door may be opening for a larger retracement, and the $3300 level is the next key spot of support I will pay attention to.

Gold (XAU/USD) Daily Chart - July 25, 2025

Key Levels to Consider:

Support

  • 3300
  • 3278
  • 3251 (100-day MA)

Resistance

  • 3350
  • 3400
  • 3425

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: JAKKS Pacific: Caught In Tariff Turmoil (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
JAKKS Pacific, Inc. (NASDAQ:JAKK) reported concerning Q2 results – the company is understandably caught in significant tariff turbulence due to sourcing from China. The toy company previously had strong operational momentum with content-led sales growth, but such momentum is now clearly behind. As the global trade environment has been quick to change and the U.S. consumer sentiment varies, JAKKS has a very turbulent outlook ahead.

I remained at a Buy rating in my previous October 2024 article, titled “JAKKS Pacific Q3: Christmas Came Early”. The stock has since lost -38% of its value due to tariff policies, significantly underperforming S&P 500’s 12% gain.

JAKKS Is Caught in Tariff Turmoil in the U.S.

As can be expected, JAKKS reported a significant earnings decline in Q2. The report showed revenues of just $119.1 million, down by -20% year-on-year and missing Wall Street’s already quite conservative consensus by $5.0 million. Earnings also showed a considerable decline, although the still positive adjusted EPS of $0.03 beat expectations by $0.20.

The quarter reflects the impact of tariffs on JAKKS in the U.S. – Trump’s current tariffs on China have caused havoc on JAKKS’ sourcing that’s predominantly based in the country, fundamentally altering JAKKS’ earnings prospects. The current nominal 55% tariff level on Chinese goods poses a massive additional cost on JAKKS, while the effective tariff level stands at a still significant but lower 30% level due to exemptions. The trade war between the U.S. and China during the April-May period, temporarily causing triple-digit percentage tariffs, likely caused temporarily even higher operational turbulence for JAKKS in Q2.

The sales performance clearly reflects hesitancy throughout the industry as JAKKS’ U.S. sales declined by -31% to $87.0 million; in the Q2 earnings call, JAKKS already noted that pricing increases have been met with consumer resistance on the retail level. The consumer sentiment has weakened, already causing more uncertain spending as JAKKS’ retailer customers are trying to manage the tariff landscape. Pricing power and consumer behavior largely remain cloudy as JAKKS’ earnings call also reflects significant uncertainty from the management. Tariff changes have essentially put a halt on JAKKS’ previously very strong momentum, led by massive content-led sales growth in Q1. The film slate still stands strong for JAKKS, which should aid the company in maintaining sales better through the 2025-2026 period in a challenging industry environment.

Positively, JAKKS’ gross margin even expanded by 80 basis points to 32.8% in Q2 despite tariff pressure; JAKKS seems to be focused on protecting margins with pricing and instead sacrificing sales volume. Content-led sales growth was noted to cause a better sales mix, but regardless, the gross profit performance is impressively strong when considering tariffs. JAKKS has a minimal inventory level, making sourcing price increases show up fast in margins. Regardless, earnings plummeted due to massive sales deleverage, and operating income declined to a -$2.8 million loss from a $7.6 million profit in Q2’24; maintaining the gross margin still comes with a massive price tag.

The U.S. market performance has minimal visibility in upcoming quarters despite a strong film slate. Tariff policies have been fast to change, making tariff mitigation efforts challenging to execute - JAKKS has tried to mitigate tariffs by switching sourcing to Vietnam and Mexico, but both countries now also face significant tariffs as well. The U.S. and China have ongoing trade talks, but a sustainable trade deal hasn’t yet been reached. JAKKS' long-term ability to shift increased tariffs onto consumers is uncertain, albeit as a silver lining, the industry as a whole relies largely on Chinese imports; with the industry basically all facing the same cost increase, JAKKS should manage to increase prices well. At the same time, consumer uncertainty adds another layer of weakness to the current outlook.

Regardless of such uncertainty, I believe that JAKKS’ very weak Q2 doesn’t reflect the company’s long-term earnings prospects. The ability to shift tariffs into pricing should improve over time. As the U.S.-China trade war escalated during Q2, JAKKS had to put a lot of business in especially costumes on pause during the quarter, but the currently already lower tariff level should enable deliveries to resume. As tariff policies stabilize, JAKKS should be able to plan sourcing changes better for the long term. For now, earnings have to be taken one quarter at a time as the outlook is very volatile. Being a bright spot in the Q2 report, JAKKS has managed to shift sales towards international markets from the U.S. market in a major initiative as international sales surged by 41% to $32.1 million.

JAKKS’ Valuation Has Very Uncertain Upside

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for JAKKS. The DCF model now reflects significant tariff pressure, causing significant pressure on JAKKS’ fair valuation.

I assume sales weakness to continue throughout 2025, albeit at a slower pace in H2 than in Q2 with a total -8.5% decline for the year. I afterwards assume sales to stabilize at quite a subdued level with 2% perpetual growth from 2026 forward.

JAKKS’ margin outlook remains highly uncertain with ongoing tariff uncertainty. In a base scenario, I assume the EBIT margin to ultimately stabilize at 5%, 70 basis points below the 2024 level. The estimate suggests that JAKKS manages to mitigate a good portion of the tariff impact throughout the next couple of years. I still expect JAKKS to convert after-tax earnings well into free cash flow.

The estimates put JAKKS’ fair value at $24.80, 31% above the stock price at the time of writing – the stock has a good amount of upside in my base scenario. Such upside remains highly speculative though – the tariff landscape is still evolving, and JAKKS’ sustainable earnings level is very hard to gauge for now. As such, I believe that the stock should be approached with caution despite the estimated base scenario upside.

The fair value estimate is down from $42.15 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 11.39%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I don’t assume JAKKS to draw a noteworthy amount of debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.42% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.62% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent July estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.40. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 11.39%.

Takeaway

JAKKS has gotten caught in tariff turmoil due to the company’s exposure to Chinese sourcing. Mitigation efforts have so far been challenging, and as the consumer reaction and the tariff landscape evolve, JAKKS has a very uncertain earnings outlook ahead. While the stock has significant upside potential if tariff pressure decreases, such upside is speculative for now. As such, while the stock has gone much lower, I downgrade my rating on JAKKS to Hold for now.

data.title: United Rentals: Overvalued Even With A Tax Law Windfall (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of United Rentals (NYSE:URI) have been an excellent performer over the past year, adding 24% as its business continues to generate durable free cash flow. URI has staged an impressive rally from the lows thanks to solid results and a significant windfall from changes in tax law. I last covered shares of United Rentals in April, rating the stock a “hold” in the hopes investors could get a better opportunity to buy at $525. Instead, I should have recommended buying shares right away, as they have surged over 60% since then as tariff fears ease and results have been strong. With shares having rallied so much and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit URI. I think the rally is overdone.

In the company’s second quarter, United Rentals earned $10.47, which was actually $0.07 below expectations. While the core rental business continues to perform fairly well, there has been a loss of profitability from normalizing used equipment prices. Still, URI has generated $1.2 billion of free cash flow in the first half of the year, and it has been aggressively returning capital to investors. Alongside earnings, it upsized its share repurchase program by $500 million to $2 billion. It did $417 million of buyback in Q2 and expects to do about $1.23 billion in H2. Thanks to buybacks, its share count was down about 2.5%. URI also has a modest 0.8% dividend yield.

Overall, United Rentals had $1.81 billion of adjusted EBITDA, and EBITDA margins compressed by 100bps to 45.9%. Normalization of used equipment prices has reduced margins and profitability over the past year. Amid post-COVID supply disruptions, used equipment prices (like used car prices) rose to unusually high levels, creating a one-time windfall, and pricing is now more consistent with long-term trends. Used equipment sales were down 13% to $317 million with an adjusted margin of 48.3%, down 350 bps from last year. I expect used equipment pricing to be relatively stable sequentially, though tariffs could provide some support if they reduce the availability of new equipment.

Aside from changes in used equipment sales, results were solid, though margins also face some pressure. Rental revenue grew by 6.2%. Its fleet was up 3.6% from last year, and URI’s fleet productivity was a solid 3.3% in Q2, up 20bps from Q1 but down from the ~4% it reported in 2024.

Specialty rentals grew by 14% to $1.15 billion, consistent with management’s efforts to grow this higher-margin segment. General rental revenue was up a more modest 2.7% to $2.27 billion. General rental margins of 35.1% were down 120 bps from last year. Meanwhile, specialty margins were 45.8%, down 220 bps. While both are down, the ~10% higher margin for more specialty equipment speaks to why URI is focused on growing its exposure here.

Still as evidenced by lower margins, the company has not been immune from slowing construction activity. Importantly, it has meaningful diversity in its client base, and URI has very little exposure to residential construction activity, where I see persistent weakness given elevated interest rates and softening home prices. There is some risk of weakening upstream oil & gas capex given where oil prices are, but with that less than 15% of its business, I view this risk as very manageable.

As you can see below, nonresidential construction activity is down nearly 3% from its high. The absolute level of spending remains elevated, supported by multiyear government projects as well as meaningful inflation. Importantly, new data center activity is supporting construction activity, which may help to replace lost government and renewable energy activity following government policy changes. Overall, I expect this to support flattish spending activity with URI’s pivot to specialty and market share gains from smaller competitors in this fragmented market essential to driving its own revenue growth.

Importantly, URI has an excellent balance sheet. It has steadily reduced its reliance on debt financing as it has built scale, which helps to insulate the business from elevated interest rates. It has $13.4 billion of debt, but no fixed rate debt matures until 2027. It now targets 1.5-2x leverage, and URI operates comfortably within this range. As such, I see management deploying all free cash flow to share repurchases, and URI is likely to pursue tuck-in acquisitions, when possible, especially in the specialty sector.

Frankly, Q2 results primarily validated that the business and its end-markets continued to operate well during a very difficult period amidst tariff uncertainty. The macro backdrop has improved with more certainty on tariffs and looser financial conditions, which should support some sequential acceleration in underlying results; however, the real upside surprise in its Q2 earnings report was management’s updated guidance particularly on free cash flow.

United Rentals’ adjusted EBITDA guidance is up $50 million at the midpoint. This is a positive move, of course, but it is not one typically associated with a ~10% share price rally. However, its free cash flow guidance is up by $400 million. URI is a major winner of the recent tax bill, which enables immediate expensing of capital expenditures. Given its large annual capex budget to replenish and grow its fleet (it has done $1.58 billion of net cap-ex so far this year), this measure is a significant cash tax savings in 2025. Management, on the earnings call, confirmed that the forward cash flow trajectory beyond 2025 should maintain the same $400 million step up.

This is a larger impact than I expected, especially in the outer-years, but management’s guidance of this being the run-rate tailwind, all else equal, was massively positive. In practice, free cash flow may not rise quite that much, as we could see URI invest more in cap-ex with these tax savings. In other words, its future cap-ex budget could be $200 million higher and free cash flow $200 million higher than otherwise, though this should still ultimately be equally positive as URI will have faster fleet and revenue growth as a result.

Essentially this tax saving is worth about $80-100 per share, using a 7% discount rate, which is basically the entirety of the rally since Wednesday. In practice, some tax savings were likely reflected in shares’ rally to that point with the modest EBITDA increase also supporting a higher share price, but the persistence of the tax savings is the primary reason for the rally. With this tax change permanent (though a future congress could pass a law to change it), I do think that whatever one thought URI was worth pre-tax law should be increased by at least $80.

Now, I was targeting $33-35 in 2025 free cash flow back in April. Today, I see the company generating about $40 in free cash flow. Still, shares are trading with just a 4.5% free cash flow yield, which is extremely expensive for an asset-heavy construction-related company. All of the forward outlooks may not be fully reflecting the tax benefit, but every metric shows URI trading at a massive premium to its own history.

While I view URI as an excellent company, I very much struggle to see it achieve its medium-term aspirational targets. In particular, I very much struggle to see URI achieving a 50% margin when even its higher-margin specialty business is below this threshold. Basically, URI would need to generate an incremental EBITDA margin of ~65% on its next $4 billion of revenue to achieve this. The company could add $4 billion of revenue between now and 2028, but I struggle to see more than $9.4 billion of EBITDA, which itself would be an ambitious 50% incremental margin.

I’ve covered URI for 7 years, but for the first time, I feel compelled to rate shares a “sell.” My view of management and the underlying company has not changed, but the stock has now reached a point where it is pricing in growth that will be profoundly difficult to achieve, especially as I do not expect meaningful end market growth. I struggle to value URI much past a 6% free cash flow yield, using of course, its higher post-tax law cash flow. That points to $675-$700. Trading at a 20% premium to fair value, I see meaningful downside risk if there is any hiccup in economic activity, market sentiment, or results. As such, I recommend selling shares and taking profits in URI after an extraordinary run.

data.title: CleanSpark: Benefiting From Bitcoin Surge
data.description:
Amid a sky-high stock market, I continue to believe that investors have to rotate more and more of our portfolios to lesser-known names in order to beat the major indices. For me, that means increasing my exposure to small-caps.

CleanSpark (NASDAQ:CLSK), in my view, is an excellent company that gives exposure to both an undervalued small-cap name plus rising Bitcoin prices. One of the most prominent Bitcoin miners in the U.S., half of CleanSpark's value alone sits in its current Bitcoin treasury, while the company continues to add to its hash rate to increase Bitcoin production.

Year to date, shares of CleanSpark have jumped ~25% - roughly mirroring the rise in Bitcoin. And yet, in my view the stock still remains quite undervalued.

I last wrote a buy opinion on CleanSpark in April, when the stock was trading near $9 per share. Since then, CleanSpark has boosted its operational throughput while also adopting a strategy of selling more Bitcoin, to take advantage of higher prices and to ensure more consistent liquidity flow. I remain at a buy rating here, though I'm cognizant of several new risks that have emerged.

Hash rate boost, increased selling

Let's now start with the positive news that CleanSpark has to report. As a reminder for investors who are newer to this name, the company reports the results of its Bitcoin mining operations every month. In July, the company reported mining results for the month of June, in which CleanSpark crossed a significant threshold: exceeding a hash rate of 50 EH/s for the first time, which was its stated goal for the mid year.

Meanwhile, during the month of June, the company produced 685 Bitcoin - on an annualized basis, this would equate to ~8,200 bitcoins produced annually.

But perhaps the most significant operational strategy change for CleanSpark is the fact that the company is now actively looking to sell more Bitcoin, rather than holding the majority of its production for investment. This is a fantastic way for CleanSpark to benefit from Bitcoin's meteoric rise this year (at the time of writing, up 25% to ~$118k since the start of January).

The company initiated what it calls a "Digital Asset Management" strategy, owned by the company's treasury team. The goal is to be able to drive spot sales of Bitcoin with "derivative overlay" in order to take advantage of rising prices. In June, the company sold 578.5 Bitcoin, or 84% of what it produced.

I view the pivot to a "produce and sell" strategy as a positive that should help decouple CleanSpark's value from the price of Bitcoin itself. Today, the company's Bitcoin treasury represents roughly half of its market cap value - to me, bringing down that treasury unlocks the possibility of buying back shares of reinvesting into additional production capacity.

Rising energy costs and less transparency

This being said, we do have to be aware of one risk: CleanSpark didn't report its usual customary "cost per Bitcoin mined" in its most recent quarter, only citing that mining costs have increased due to rising energy prices. The company most recently reported $34,000 per coin mined in Q1 (the December quarter), and $36,250 in Q4 (September). We had been hopeful for a falling cost trajectory, but now we know only that Q2's (March quarter) mining cost was above what it had been in Q1.

Per CEO Zach Bradford's remarks on the Q2 earnings call in May on mining costs and mining difficulty:

Average revenue per Bitcoin was up 10.5% quarter over quarter and nearly 69% year over year, while our marginal cost per coin rose, reflecting both increased network difficulty and higher nationwide power prices. We remained focused on margin and long-term performance rather than any single metric.

I want to be clear on what that means. That means we do not manage the power prices, and when margins are healthy, we run through slightly elevated power prices, as long as this drives more value to the bottom line. As a result, the average power price printed higher while delivering increased gross profit [...]

Mining difficulty rose 3.6% during the quarter, while power costs increased. Yet, thanks to improved fleet efficiency, our gross margin compression nearly matched the difficulty change, demonstrating our ability to absorb external pressures through operational gains. Our approach has never been about chasing the lowest cost per kilowatt hour. Instead, we managed to margin, making deliberate decisions, including running through higher price periods when doing so generates positive cash flow."

Still, at least for now, we take comfort in the fact that the rise in Bitcoin price should dramatically outweigh the rise in energy prices. If, taken together, increased mining difficulty plus higher electricity cost yields a ~10% sequential jump in cost per coin (from ~$34k in Q4 to ~$37k today), that gap is still more than covered by Bitcoin's rise from ~$100k in May to $118k today.

We note one additional risk: in spite of the company's boost in hash rate to 50 EH/s, CleanSpark's monthly coin production has actually not increased (thanks to rising mining difficulty, which will always be the case and is at the heart of Bitcoin's scarcity). The 685 coins that the company produced in June was -1% lower than 694 coins produced in May (but higher than 633 in April).

Valuation scenarios

Now, we turn to a perennially difficult question to answer about CleanSpark: how do we value this company? It's an exercise that can be done, but one that rests on a number of critical assumptions. These are the key levers of my pro forma P&L:

  • Mining throughput: 8,220 Bitcoin per year (based on current 685 monthly production, annualized)
  • Cost per coin: $40,000 (a conservative figure based on management's directional commentary on rising difficulty and energy costs, relative to $34,000 most recently reported in Q1)
  • Gross margin per coin: $75,000 or ~65% (based on $115k selling price and $40k cost per coin), or $616 million in gross profit dollars per year
  • Opex: $136 million (based on 15% y/y growth versus $118.1 million in costs of payroll, G&A, and professional fees in FY24. The growth rate in opex is roughly in-line with the company's growth in hash rate).

This would give us a rough adjusted EBITDA of $480 million ($616 million in gross profit dollars, less $136 million in opex excluding depreciation and amortization as above).

Of course, a lot here is truly beyond CleanSpark's control, including and especially Bitcoin prices, but also energy prices and mining difficulty. It's prudent for us to stress-test the company's profitability based on a range of different Bitcoin price outcomes.

Holding mining cost constant at $40k/coin:

  • At BTC of $100,000, gross profit per coin falls to $60k, gross profit per year falls to $493 million, and adjusted EBITDA falls to $357 million (-26% less than my "base case" using current BTC prices).
  • At BTC of $80,000 (roughly where BTC dipped when the market dropped in April), gross profit per coin falls to $40k, gross profit per year falls to $329 million, and adjusted EBITDA falls to $193 million.

Needless to say, we think CleanSpark can remain quite profitable against a wide range of Bitcoin price outcomes, and its valuation still remains modest. At current share prices near $12, the stock trades at a $3.82 billion market cap. After we net off the 12,608 of BTC holdings in its latest June report (worth $1.49 billion at current market values), alongside $97 million of cash and $647 million of debt on the company's latest Q2 balance sheet, the company's rough resulting enterprise value is $1.78 billion.

Against a "doomsday" scenario of ~$80k BTC prices, the stock still trades at only ~9x adjusted EBITDA, whereas in the "base case" scenario at current BTC prices of $118k, the company trades at <4x adjusted EBITDA.

Risks and key takeaways

We'll emphasize here that CleanSpark is, of course, a very risky investment. The greatest driver of its profitability is Bitcoin prices - something that has proven very volatile and is completely outside of CleanSpark's control. We're also watchful of the fact that CleanSpark's rising hash rate has not really translated into a meaningful jump in monthly coin production, and we're also looking for more transparency on what rising energy prices means from a mining cost perspective.

That being said, in spite of the uncertainty, I think CleanSpark remains quite substantially undervalued, thanks to its Bitcoin treasury and the sharp recent jump in Bitcoin prices. Stay long here.

data.title: Fidelity Short-Term Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Taxable Bond Market Review

U.S.taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.21% in the second quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, amid heightened volatility driven largely by market uncertainty around U.S. trade and fiscal policy. Year to date, the index has gained 4.02%.

The quarter got off to a dramatic start on April 2, when President Trump- proclaiming the day as Liberation Day-announced severe "reciprocal" tariffs on virtually all the U.S.'s trade partners, which he said were effective immediately. This led to a sharp but short-term selloff in the U.S. bond market, as prices fell and yields, which move inversely to prices, rose. Between April 4 and April 11, the yield on the benchmark 10-year U.S. Treasury rose from 4.01% to 4.48%, an extraordinary one-week spike. But the market stabilized shortly after the administration announced a 90-day pause on the tariffs (except those on China) on April 9. Notably, the yield on the 10-year Treasury closed the month exactly where it began, at 4.17%. The Aggregate index advanced a modest 0.39% for the month.

The index then returned -0.72% in May. During the month, investors became worried about the nation's fiscal trajectory as the administration's tax bill made its way through Congress. If passed in its current form, the budget legislation, dubbed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, is expected to increase the national deficit by $2.4 trillion over the next ten years, according to the Congressional Budget Office. By late May, long-term (20- and 30-year) Treasury yields had risen above 5%, reflecting concerns about potentially persistent inflation and debt sustainability. On May 16, Moody's (MCO) became the last of the three large U.S. credit-rating agencies to downgrade U.S. Treasury debt, following similar actions by Standard & Poor's and Fitch in 2011 and 2023, respectively. All three now give U.S. debt their second-highest rating. The market, however, rallied in June, rising 1.54%, helped by stable monthly inflation metrics and stronger-than-expected employment data. These developments contributed to a growing expectation that the U.S. Federal Reserve would resume its monetary easing program and implement policy interest-rate cuts in the second half of 2025, after pausing in the year's first half. Treasury yields declined across most of the curve during the month, with intermediate segments seeing the largest moves. For the full quarter, the yield curve steepened, as shorter rates fell slightly, the 10-Year Treasury was flat, and longer rates edged up.

Against this backdrop, intermediate bonds with maturities from three to seven years saw the strongest price performance, while investment-grade securities of 10 years or more declined slightly amid rising yields. By credit rating, bonds rated BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB) outperformed higher-quality issues.

Results at the sector level reflected an increased late-quarter appetite for risk, with yield-advantaged credit sectors outperforming Treasurys (+0.85%).U.S. corporate bonds (+1.82%) and commercial mortgage-backed securities (+1.88%) topped the investment-grade performance chart, bolstered by tightening credit spreads and resilient fundamentals, followed by government-related securities (+1.70%), asset-backed securities (+1.38%) and agency mortgage-backed securities (+1.14%).

Outside the Aggregate index, U.S. Treasury inflation protected securities lagged (+0.48%), per Bloomberg, while U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (+3.53%) and emerging-markets high-income securities (+3.75%) substantially outpaced their investment-grade counterparts, as spreads narrowed sharply later in the quarter as economic sentiment improved.

Fund Positioning (Impact vs. Benchmark)

● Within the financials sector, picks among banks contributed.(Positive)

Outlook and Positioning

At the midpoint of 2025, the global economic cycle remained in expansion, although it's become less synchronized across countries and more uncertain amid a variety of policy crosscurrents. The U.S. demonstrated a wide mix of dynamics, including solid mid-cycle indicators as well as signs of softening activity.

Outside the U.S., monetary easing provided a tailwind, as many central banks continued to cut interest rates, including the eurozone and U.K. The U.S. Federal Reserve held its policy rate steady, but at quarter end the market expected cuts totaling 0.5% to 0.75% (50 to 75 basis points) in the year's second half. Inflation remained above the Fed's 2% target throughout Q2.

Despite high volatility during the quarter, nominal 10-year U.S.Treasury yields finished about where they began, around 4.2%.Overall, yields remained modestly lower than where they started 2025, with real yields- the inflation-adjusted cost of borrowing- still at the high end of their range over the past decade. The U.S. dollar dropped to a three-year low in Q2, extending its year-to-date decline to more than 10%, per the ICE U.S. Dollar Index. As of June 30, about 42% of the fund's assets were invested in corporate bonds. Within corporates, financials remained our largest sector allocation, at roughly 26% of total assets. The portfolio also owned 34% U.S. Treasurys and a 17% non-benchmark allocation to asset-backed securities, including securitized car loan debt and collateralized loan obligations. Consistent with our strategy, the fund was notably underweight U.S. Treasurys, which represented 71% of the benchmark at quarter end.

In a dynamic environment, we continue to find pockets of value within the fixed-income market, based on our view of pricing and fundamentals. Our goal remains to work with our investment teams to try to find attractively priced bonds for the portfolio while maintaining a disciplined approach to risk management.

It is important to reiterate that the portfolio is constructed with a careful emphasis on security selection, especially with consideration to liquidity and financial resiliency. Investing is a long-term endeavor, and we're focused on generating strong risk-adjusted performance over a full market cycle.

data.title: Fidelity Limited Term Bond Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Taxable Bond Market Review

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 2.78% in the first quarter of 2025, according to the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, as market uncertainty around the possibility of steep tariffs by the U.S. on its foreign trade partners weighed on domestic stocks and led some investors to seek haven in fixed-income assets, particularly U.S. Treasurys.

In the previous quarter, bond yields rose markedly, while prices, which move in the opposite direction of yields, retreated.Though the U.S. stock market responded favorably to the election outcome on November 5- and to the prospect of potential tax cuts and deregulation under a second Trump administration - the bond market was concerned with the possibility that certain proposed policy changes might exacerbate already stubborn inflation. In mid-December 2024, the U.S. Federal Reserve acknowledged that the pace of the monetary easing it began in September would be dependent on further progress toward its inflation target and penciled in just two further interest-rate reductions for 2025. As of December 31, the nominal 10-year U.S. Treasury yield stood at 4.58%, having climbed 77 basis points (0.77%) in Q4.

In contrast, the 10-year yield fell 35 bps in the first quarter, closing Q1 at 4.23%. Historically, U.S. Treasury yields tend to drop when economic uncertainty rises- and uncertainty rose sharply during the three months. Equity markets whipsawed as President Trump and members of the administration adopted alternately aggressive and soothing rhetoric about the President's plans. On March 4, the administration implemented tariffs on certain goods from Mexico and Canada, only to postpone them two days later. As of quarter end, the President had declared April 2 as "Liberation Day"- that is, the day that the full shape and scale of his trade policy plans would be announced. As stock and bond markets reacted, the overall trend was a reversal from the risk-on environment of Q4 to a risk-off mindset in Q1 of the new year. By month, the Aggregate index advanced a modest 0.53% in January, when U.S. stocks were still on the rise but a tame inflation report quelled rising bond yields; it then gained 2.20% in February, as movement by investors to de-risk out of equities and into fixed income gained steam; and was essentially flat (+0.04%) in March, as market participants awaited the April announcement on tariffs.The Fed, for its part, maintained its "wait and see" stance during the quarter, pausing further action on policy interest rates.

Against a backdrop of falling yields, fixed-income categories with greater rate sensitivity enjoyed the best price gains. Long-term (10+year) debt issues advanced 3.40% and outpaced intermediate- and short-term issues, with returns weakest at the front end (1-3 years) of the curve (+1.63%). By credit rating, bonds rated AA had a notable performance advantage over highest-quality AAA bonds and lower-quality investment-grade securities rated A and BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB).

Results at the sector level reflected a flight to quality, with U.S.Treasurys (+2.92%) and the high-quality mortgage-backed securities segment (+3.06%) outperforming higher-risk categories such as U.S. investment-grade corporate bonds (+2.31%), asset-backed securities (+1.53%) and commercial mortgage-backed securities (+2.57%) on a total return basis.

Outside the Aggregate index, U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities gained a robust 4.17%, per Bloomberg, while U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (+.00%) and emerging-markets high-income securities (+.60%) lagged amid declining yields and widening spreads for credit-based assets.

Overall, sector positioning contributed to the fund's performance versus the Fidelity Limited Term Composite Index.

● Favoring short-term corporate bonds aided relative performance. (Positive)

● An underweight in U.S. Treasurys and government-backed bonds detracted.(Negative)

● Non-benchmark allocations to securitized debt-including asset- backed securities, mortgage-backed securities and commercial mortgage- backed securities- contributed. (Positive)
Strategy: Duration and Yield Curve Strategy: Duration and Yield Curve

Fund Positioning (Impact vs. Index)

Yield-curve positioning slightly detracted from relative performance.

● The fund's overall duration was generally in line with that of the benchmark.(Neutral)

Strategy: Security Selection

Fund Positioning (lmpact vs. Index)

Security selection in investment-grade bonds aided relative performance the past three months.

● Selection among financial institutions, especially banks, contributed.(Positive)

● Investment choices within the industrial sector also helped. (Positive)

Outlook and Positioning

The U.S. Federal Reserve kept its policy rate steady during Q1 after cutting rates by one percentage point (100 basis points) during 2024. Until late last year, the market expected an additional 150 bps of easing in 2025. But persistently strong inflation, elevated inflation expectations from consumer surveys, and uncertainty over upcoming U.S. tariffs caused the Fed to reduce its outlook to only two more cuts, likely of 25 bps each, this year.

Over the past three months, trends across major global economies became less synchronized as policy decisions diverged. Specifically, uncertainty about the direction of U.S. policy weighed on financial markets, with investors digesting news about a flurry of executive actions, cuts to government staffing and programs, tighter immigration controls and, most notably, the new, higher tariff rates, which are expected to be unveiled on April 2.

This heightened uncertainty prompted a move by investors away from U.S. stocks and toward greater diversification in fixed income, gold, and non-U.S. assets. At quarter end, near-term recession risks appear relatively low, but growth risks have shifted to the downside.

As of March 31, about 63% of the fund's assets were invested in corporate bonds. Within corporates, the debt of financial institutions and industrial companies were roughly 31% and 28% of total assets, respectively. The portfolio also owned about 18% U.S. Treasurys at quarter end, and a roughly 9% non-composite allocation to asset-backed securities, such as collateralized loan obligations and securitized car loan debt. Consistent with the fund's strategy, the portfolio was underweight U.S. Treasurys, which represented about 20% of the Composite index and a considerably larger percentage of the fund's Bloomberg benchmark.

To learn more about how the fund was positioned versus the Fidelity Limited Term Composite Index, see the tables below.

In a dynamic environment, we continue to find pockets of value within the fixed-income market. Our goal remains to work with our investment teams to try to find attractively priced bonds for the portfolio while maintaining a disciplined approach to risk management. It is important to reiterate that the portfolio is constructed with a careful emphasis on security selection, especially with consideration to liquidity and financial resiliency. Investing is a long-term endeavor, and we're focused on generating strong risk-adjusted performance over a full market cycle.

FUND INFORMATION

In general the bond market is volatile, and fixed income securities carry interest rate risk.(As interest rates rise, bond prices usually fall, and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer-term securities.) Fixed income securities also carry inflation risk and credit and default risks for both issuers and counterparties. Unlike individual bonds, most bond funds do not have a maturity date, so avoiding losses caused by price volatility by holding them until maturity is not possible. Lower-quality debt securities involve greater risk of default or price changes due to potential changes in the credit quality of the issuer. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate,economic, and political risks, all of which may be magnified in emerging markets.Leverage can increase market exposure and magnify investment risk.

Investment Approach

Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. 30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. data.title: Fidelity Strategic Dividend & Income Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. stocks gained 10.94% in the second quarter, according to the S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX))* index, ending the period at an all-time high after erasing a brief but steep early-April decline that followed President Trump's announcement of a sweeping tariff plan. Amid rising volatility and concern about a global trade war, the index returned -12.12% from April 2 through April 8, but then sharply reversed course and rose 24.92% through midyear. Stocks entered Q2 in a tailspin, as post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities. After achieving a then-record close on February 19, the S&P 500® reached the brink of a bear market- a decline of at least 20%- as it returned -18.75% through April 8.Gains of 6.29% in May and 5.09% in June spearheaded the 11-week rally, with investors upbeat entering the second half of the year.

The historically fast rebound was set off by a 90-day pause on most planned tariffs, and sustained by robust corporate profits, a solid economy and inflation trending around the Federal Reserve's 2% target. These tailwinds overshadowed rising tension in the Middle East and lingering uncertainty about trade ahead of the July 8 expiration of the tariff pause. Unless new deals are reached, historic tariff increases could be reinstated on goods from Europe, Japan, and other countries/regions. The Federal Reserve held steady its benchmark federal funds rate in May and June, as expected, stating it is open to cutting rates in the second half of the year but prefers a wait-and-see stance for the time being, given its expectation for meaningful inflation in the coming months, as well as uncertainty about the impact of tariffs on prices..

For the quarter, large-caps bested small-caps, while growth (+19%) topped value (+3%) within the S&P 500*. Consequently, the index's gain was concentrated in the growth-oriented information technology (+24%) and communication services (+18%) sectors, mostly reflecting the potential for generative artificial intelligence to drive transformative change. Consumer discretionary (+12%) stocks also fared well. In total, these three groups represented about half of the index this period. Firms in the industrials sector, which typically move in step with the economy, rose 13%.In contrast, energy companies (-9%) lagged most, as the price of oil fell in Q2. Two defensive sectors also lost ground: health care (-7%) and real estate (-0.07%). Consumer staples (+%) and utilities (+4%), both considered defensive, underperformed as well. Financials stocks moderated after a post-election boost from expectations for easing regulation within the sector. Lastly, materials advanced 3%.

Within this environment, the Fidelity Strategic Dividend& Income Composite IndexSM was up 1.51% the past three months. The index consists of four asset classes: high-dividend-yielding stocks, preferred stocks, real estate investment trusts and convertible securities. Dividend-paying equities, as measured by the MSCI (MSCI) USA High Dividend Yield Index, advanced 0.35% this quarter, propelled by information technology, financials and industrials stocks, whereas energy fared the worst. Meanwhile, REITs, according to the FTSE NAREIT Equity REITs Index, returned 1.16%. After volatility early in Q2, investors saw real estate equities rally. With a continued-strong labor market, both residential real estate and office space continued to experience steady demand. Industrial RElTs lagged due to the impact of tariffs, particularly in port-focused regions. Elsewhere, convertible securities-hybrid instruments that combine the features of both stocks and bonds- gained 8.88% this period, per the ICE BofA All U.S. Convertibles Index, while preferred stocks, as measured by the ICE BofA® Fixed Rate Preferred Securities index, rose 0.93%.■

Outlook and Positioning

The fund employs a lead portfolio-manager/subportfolio-manager structure. The two co-lead portfolio managers make the allocation decisions across the fund's major asset classes. Individual selection decisions within each of the asset classes are made by experienced and specialized managers within their respective areas of expertise.Our asset allocation investment process begins with strategic allocation across the fund's asset classes. Depending on the co-lead portfolio managers' outlook for each type of investment, they may over- or underweight an asset class versus the neutral mix. This is done judiciously, with an eye toward long-term trends and in a strategic manner to help preserve the integrity of the portfolios.

Separate from the asset allocation investment process, each subportfolio has its own dedicated portfolio manager who employs an asset class-specific process for security selection. Our investment approach aims to offer a well-diversified strategy with the potential to deliver strong income regardless of the interest-rate environment.Based on Fidelity research, we have established a target mix of 50% dividend-yielding stocks, 20% preferred stocks, 15% convertible securities and 15% REITs. As the fund's neutral positioning, this is the combination of asset classes and weightings that over time we believe should provide the most favorable risk/reward for our shareholders. Our approach to managing the portfolio always is highly tactical, meaning we make shifts to the asset mix based on where we see opportunities in the marketplace at any given time.

The fund's investable universe also includes non-Composite index asset classes- including master limited partnerships and infrastructure stocks- that provide us with additional opportunities to broaden the portfolio's dividend-paying-equity exposure.

Average non-Composite exposure to infrastructure securities stood}$$at $3%$ at the midpoint of 2025, while we also maintained a small out-of-index allocation to MLPs totaling about 0.5% of net assets, on average, this quarter.

In Q2, we implemented no changes to the portfolio's target weights. Despite market volatility early in the period, we continued to see the best relative value opportunities in convertible securities and infrastructure equities, which represented the fund's largest overweights as of June 30. With convertibles, we appreciated the broadening out of the market, which we think may lead to better total-return opportunities down the road. Meanwhile, the infrastructure asset class remained attractive in our view, particularly relative to preferred securities and REITs. Overall, our positioning in these two areas proved beneficial in Q2, as the convertibles and infrastructure asset classes far outperformed the portfolio's other categories.

As of midyear, it has proven beneficial to be overweight risk assets within the portfolio, given the spread widening investors have encountered. Although the uncertainty surrounding tariffs is not yet behind us, we suspect the magnitude of the April shock is unlikely to be repeated. Add to that the corporate-friendly budget bill, which was set for passage as of quarter end, and we see the pieces in place to further support risk assets.

At the same time, we are mindful that market participants have recognized this more favorable backdrop by way of elevated stock valuations and tighter corporate-bond spreads. As always, we continually monitor the investment environment to determine whether further asset allocation adjustments may be warranted. Moreover, we will not hesitate to respond decisively if the market backdrop meaningfully changes and new asset classes begin to offer compelling value.

Stock markets, especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments. Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk(as interest rates rise bond prices usually fall), the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk and inflation risk. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate,currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. Changes in real estate values or economic downturns can have a significant negative effect on issuers in the real estate industry. Lower-quality bonds can be more volatile and have greater risk of default than higher-quality bonds. Value stocks can perform differently than other types of stocks and can continue to be undervalued by the market for long periods of time.

Investment Approach

● Fidelity Strategic Dividend & Income Fund is a multi-asset-class strategy that seeks to provide reasonable income, and potentially also capital appreciation, by investing in a diversified mix of dividend-oriented equity and hybrid securities.

● The fund's assets are allocated among high dividend-yielding stocks, preferred stocks, real estate investment trusts(REITs) and convertible securities, using a target weighting of 50%, 20%, 15%and 15%, respectively. This strategic allocation attempts to take advantage of the low correlation among these equity/hybrid classes with a goal of optimizing total returns while containing volatility over time.

● Specialized subportfolio managers are responsible for security selection in their respective areas of expertise and represent the primary source of alpha(risk-adjusted excess return), while the lead portfolio managers have the flexibility to make tactical allocation shifts around the target mix to help manage risk and capitalize on relative-value opportunities.

data.title: Fidelity Balanced Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. stocks returned -4.27% in the first three months of 2025, according to the S&P 500 (SP500), (SPX)* index, as post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs. Amid rising volatility, the index gained 4.64% through February 19, but then sharply reversed course and returned-8.51% through quarter end, particularly dragged down by growth stocks and smaller-caps.

On January 29, the Federal Reserve paused following three consecutive rate cuts beginning in September, extending its wait-and-see phase and indicating further reductions would depend on "real progress on inflation" or unexpected weakness in labor. The central bank affirmed this stance at its March 19 meeting, when it held steady its benchmark federal funds rate as it assessed sweeping policy changes and the effect on the economy. About a week later, new data showed that inflation climbed 2.8% in February from a year earlier- higher than the Fed's 2% target, more than economists expected and exceeding January's annual pace- as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers slipped, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy's path ahead.

After gaining 25.02% in 2024, the S&P 500° rose 2.78% in the opening month of the new year, as investors reacted favorably to the definitive election outcome and potential policy changes, including the prospect of tax cuts, expansionary fiscal policy and lower regulation. Post-election momentum lost steam in mid-February, as the index returned-1.30% for the month.The downtrend steepened in March (-5.63%), when the index slid into a correction by declining 10% from its February peak. For the quarter, growth stocks (-8%) within the S&P 500* notably lagged their value (+0.3%) counterparts. The index's decline was narrowly concentrated in the growth-oriented consumer discretionary (-14%) and information technology (-13%) sectors, as well as communication services (-6%). These three groups combined represented about half of the index for the three months. In contrast, energy stocks rose 10% to lead the way, even as the price of oil declined. Four defensive sectors also gained: health care (+7%), consumer staples (+5%), utilities (+5%) and real estate (+4%). Financials advanced roughly 4%, boosted by expectations for easing regulation within the sector, materials gained 3% and industrials was roughly flat.

Turning to fixed income, U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 2.78% in Q1, according to the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, as market uncertainty about possible steep tariffs by the U.S. on its foreign trade partners weighed on domestic stocks and led some investors to seek haven in fixed-income assets, particularly U.S. Treasurys. Against a backdrop of falling yields, fixed-income categories with greater sensitivity to interest rates enjoyed the best price gains. Long-term (10+ year) debt issues outpaced intermediate- and short-term issues. By credit rating, bonds rated AA had a notable performance advantage over highest-quality AAA bonds and lower-quality investment-grade securities.

Results at the sector level reflected a flight to quality, with U.S.Treasurys (+2.92%) and the high-quality mortgage-backed securities segment (+3.06%) outperforming higher-risk categories, including U.S. investment-grade corporate bonds (+2.31%), asset-backed securities (+1.53%) and commercial mortgage-backed securities (+2.57%) on a total-return basis. Outside the index, U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities gained 4.17%, per Bloomberg, while U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (+.00%) and emerging-markets high-income securities (+.60%) lagged amid declining yields and widening spreads for credit-based assets.

Performance Review

For the first quarter of 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares returned -2.74%, trailing the -1.45% result of the Fidelity Balanced Hybrid Composite IndexSM.

Within the portfolio, equities represented about 65% of assets, on average, for the first three months of the year, versus the 60% average stock allocation in the Composite index. Meanwhile, investment-grade bonds comprised about 35% of the fund's assets, on average, compared with the average Composite index weighting of 40% this quarter. The remainder of the portfolio was comprised of a cash position of less than 1%. The fund did not hold any high-yield bonds this period. Q1 was a risk-off quarter, and as a result, investment-grade bonds outperformed stocks. This meant that the fund's outsized exposure to the latter and underweight in bonds hurt, although it aided performance on a one-year basis. With that said, security selection among the fund's equity investments was the biggest detractor versus the Composite index. On the other hand, investment choices among investment-grade bonds added modest added value.

The fund's equity securities returned -5.61% for the three months, versus -4.27% for the S&P 500* index. Security selection in the information technology sector detracted most, followed by picks in health care and communication services. On an individual basis, an underweight stake in Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (+17%), a comparatively defensive stock and index component- hurt the portfolio's relative result most. In February, the insurance-focused conglomerate reported record quarterly operating earnings for the fourth quarter of 2024, up 71% and notably higher than the consensus estimate. Management attributed the strength to the company's core insurance underwriting business, especially Geico. Berkshire ended 2024 with $321.4 billion in cash and U.S. Treasurys, also a record.

Out-of-benchmark exposure to Marvell Technology (-44%) pressured performance. In early March, the provider of data infrastructure and semiconductor solutions reported slightly better-than-expected fiscal Q4 earnings. However, in an environment of high valuations and elevated expectations for Al-related solutions providers, analysts said that the company's quarterly datacenter business results - which were in line with consensus views - likely proved somewhat disappointing and contributed to the stock's decline. That said, much of the downward pressure on shares came earlier in the year, when a Chinese generative artificial intelligence startup, DeepSeek, released a free large language model that was cheaper to develop, raising questions about spending on Al infrastructure and the valuations of the biggest players, including chipmakers.

On the plus side, investment choices among energy and materials firms slightly contributed versus the composite index. In stock-specific terms, comparatively light exposure to Broadcom (AVGO) (-28%) led the way in Q1. Shares of the semiconductor firm fell sharply on January 27 after a Chinese artificial intelligence startup, DeepSeek, released a free large language model that was cheaper to develop than others on the market, raising questions about massive spending on Al infrastructure and the valuations of the biggest players, including chipmakers. The stock slipped further in advance of Broadcom's early-March announcement of financial results for the quarter ending February 2, which surprisingly topped analysts' consensus estimates for both revenue and earnings, driven by the company's Al semiconductor solutions and infrastructure software.

An overweight position Boston Scientific (BSX) also was beneficial from a relative return standpoint. The stock rose 13% for the three months, gaining ground through the midpoint of the quarter, when the medical device company released financial results that were better than Wall Street analysts had anticipated. Management attributed the favorable outcome to strong sales in its cardiovascular division,and particularly the successful launch of its Farapulse pulsed field ablation system, which relies on electrical pulses to treat abnormal heart rhythm in patients. The stock was among the portfolio's larger holdings on March 31.

Turning to fixed income, the fund's investment-grade bond holdings recorded a gain of 2.99% for Q1, topping the 2.78% advance of the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. In a quarter where the U.S. Federal Reserve paused its interest-rate cuts begun last September, Treasury yields moved sharply lower, which was a tailwind for bond prices. The Fed continued to pencil in two rate cuts for 2025 overall.Meanwhile, credit spreads ended the quarter still relatively tight on a historical basis.

Versus the Bloomberg index, security selection contributed to performance for the quarter. On a sector basis, positioning among corporate bonds notably helped, led by an underweight in industrials. Comparatively smaller exposure to mortgage-backed securities also added value. Yield-curve position proved advantageous as well, as the portfolio's duration was modestly longer than that of the index, which was helpful in a period where Treasury yields declined.

Outlook and Positioning

Macroeconomic uncertainty, which we've noted in previous reports, increased this quarter, with a tariff battle in the mix as of March 31.It's hard to handicap how this will end, but we note that during President Trump's first term, tariffs were a major issue for more than a year. Our hope is that the initial tariff announcements are intended to bring other nations to the bargaining table and not indicative of what the landscape will look like over the long term.

Against this backdrop, our equity co-managers continue to favor high-quality businesses with idiosyncratic drivers and the pricing power to offset increases in both labor and materials costs, thus enabling them to maintain their profit margins.

We made minor adjustments to the fund's equity sector weightings in Q1 but continued to pursue our essentially sector-neutral approach to investing, which allows for meaningful overweights and underweights at the industry and individual stock levels. We remain confident in our investment process, in which a team of sector-focused co-managers are charged with adding value through stock selection. Meanwhile, a group leader is responsible for general team oversight, asset allocation, cash management and risk monitoring.

We also follow a very disciplined and methodical process when it comes to the fund's fixed income holdings. We start with broad risk analysis and a search for value in the bond market, taking a top-down perspective. Once we decide how much risk to take in the portfolio, the next step is to decide which sectors we want to invest in, based on our analysis of the risk/reward tradeoff. Helping us with this effort are various fixed-income specialists at Fidelity, who regularly provide risk assessments for virtually every investable fixed-income sector we would consider, both in the U.S. and abroad. The last part of the process is picking individual bonds.We scrutinize the fund's investment universe, looking for securities we think are worth purchasing, based on our view of fundamentals,as well as supply and demand dynamics.

At quarter end, the two largest stock-specific overweights were Boston Scientific and Micron Technology (MU). Outside of the S&P 500*, the fund's most prominent equity stakes were Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing and UCB, a biopharmaceutical firm based in Belgium. Conversely, Apple, Microsoft and Berkshire Hathaway were the three biggest individual underweights as of March 31. However, the first two were core equity holdings in the portfolio.

As for investment-grade bonds, the yield curve has been broadly normalizing, and we expect further movement in that direction, along with continued moderation of inflation and a slowing U.S. economy. As of period end, the portfolio continued to carry a modest overweight in U.S. Treasurys. Outsized exposure to asset-back securities, commercial mortgage-backed securities and corporates is noteworthy as well. On the other hand, the fund carried a sizable underweight in agency MBS. Within the corporate sector, the fund had an outsized allocation to intermediate investment-grade credits with an underweight among longer-dated credits of that type. The portfolio maintained a moderately longer duration versus the benchmark, and a bulleted structure as it relates to intermediate maturities.

Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk (as interest rates rise bond prices usually fall), the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk and inflation risk. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. Lower-quality bonds can be more volatile and have greater risk of default than higher-quality bonds.Leverage can increase market exposure and magnify investment risk.

Investment Approach

● Fidelity Balanced Fund invests across a mix of stocks and bonds- with a 60%/ 40% neutral allocation- in seeking income and capital growth.

● The fund is managed by multiple portfolio managers, with each member dedicated to a particular asset class or market segment, plus a lead manager responsible for determining the appropriate asset allocation, general team oversight, cash management and risk monitoring.

● The equity subportfolio is largely sector-neutral and run by a team of sector-focused managers who look to add value through active stock selection.

● The investment-grade bond strategy seeks outperformance through sector and security selection and yield-curve positioning. The fund also has a dedicated high-yield bond portfolio, considered out-of-benchmark.

● Focused sector and asset class expertise, supported by our deep research infrastructure, is combined with disciplined portfolio construction to provide investment-process consistency in seeking to deliver attractive risk-adjusted returns over time.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio (IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings (P/E) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity (ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity.Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share Growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

FIXED INCOME CHARACTERISTICS

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features (e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as "SEC 30-Day Yield" or "standardized yield".

30-Day SEC Restated Yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

Net Asset Value is the dollar value of one share of a fund; it is determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.

IMPORTANT FUND INFORMATION

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark(index) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

INDICES

It is not possible to invest directly in an index. All indices represented are unmanaged. All indices include reinvestment of dividends and interest income unless otherwise noted.

Fidelity Balanced Hybrid Composite Index is a customized blend of unmanaged indexes,weighted as follows: S&P 500 Index-60%; and Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index-40%.

S&P 500 Index is a market capitalization-weighted index of 500 common stocks chosen for market size, liquidity, and industry group representation to represent U.S. equity performance.

Balanced Equity Blend represents the performance of the S&P 500 Index since October 01, 2008. The composition differed in periods prior to this date.

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based, market-value-weighted benchmark that measures the performance of the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. Sectors in the index include Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS, and CMBS.

MARKET-SEGMENT WEIGHTS

Market-segment weights illustrate examples of sectors or industries in which the fund may invest, and may not be representative of the fund's current or future investments. They should not be construed or used as a recommendation for any sector or industry.

RANKING INFORMATION
